 306        S. JAKOB

characteristic ampliﬁes the human aspect (of shame and the ambition to
succeed professionally and personally) within this transnational problem.
It can also inform a change in the ‘common sense’ of corporate regulation
from within a corporation, by connecting the employee to the reputation
of the company and its economic success.
    Furthermore, the rating of a company’s business partners has an impact
on the company’s score87 and thus provides an incentive to the corporation
to monitor the behaviour of their business partners.88 This incentive could
also be present for business partners operating outside of the PRC. For
example, if such a business partner treats Taiwan as a sovereign country,
this could be judged by the CCP, alias the algorithm, as contravening trust-
worthiness according to the Chinese perspective and this in turn would not
just concern this particular company but might inﬂuence the rating of its
partner in the PRC. Companies oﬀering online services across borders
might be aﬀected as well. Zoom Video Communications decided to interrupt
virtual meetings in the memory of the Tiananmen massacre and deleted
accounts of participants due to the pressure of Chinese state oﬃcials who
regard the virtual meetings as illegal.89 Since there was no technical possi-
bility to end the session of only those participants with an IP address from
mainland China, the sessions of the participants in the US and Hong
Kong were also shut down.90 Whereas this particular example is not con-
nected to the CSCS, it shows that transnational eﬀects occur already when
it comes to values and ideology in the law. ‘Insubordinate businesses […]
carry political risks. Yet, the political and economic necessities of
cooperation [with the Chinese government] often outweigh their dissen-
sion’.91 Hence, this example shows that it is an established theme of the
CCP to coerce companies into complying with the expectations and state
rational of the CCP and that it is an established aim to enhance the CCP’s
ability to control the discourse outside China’s borders. The crucial point
is that the CSCS broadens the possibilities for the CCP to do so92 and
